coredata.dc:title: Structure-dependent lithium storage characteristics of Fe3O4/rGO aerogels
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fei
Wen, Xinyue
Mittal, Uttam
Nekouei, Rasoul Khayyam
Foller, Tobias
Shang, Yuan
Bhadra, Abhirup
Chu, Dewei
Sharma, Neeraj
Kundu, Dipan
Joshi, Rakesh
coredata.dc:description: Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) not only shows distinctive electrochemical properties desirable for battery performance but also presents functionalities that enable the production of free-standing electrodes and allow further functionalization to form composites. When combined with Fe3O4 which possesses a high theoretical capacity, Fe3O4/rGO composites demonstrate enhanced battery performance compared to their individual form. Despite numerous efforts invested in developing high-performance Fe3O4/rGO Li-ion battery anodes, fundamental questions regarding structure-dependent lithium storage behaviour have been lacking experimental support in detail. In this work, we report the synthesis of free-standing Fe3O4/rGO that is highly porous with tunable features to increase the availability of electroactive sites, channels for ion passage, and space for Li accommodation, thus enhancing Li storage capacity and prolonging cycle life. As-prepared Fe3O4/rGO electrodes deliver a high reversible capacity of 1224 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and a retention rate of 82.8% after 1000 cycles at 1 A g−1. These Fe3O4/rGO composites with desirable features, hence, hold promise in applications as anodes in high-power lithium-ion batteries.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4/rGO aerogels
Lithium-ion batteries
Pore engineering
Cycling durability

The authors acknowledge the facilities and the scientific and technical assistance of Microscopy Australia at the Electron Microscope Unit (EMU), Spectroscopy Laboratory and Solid State & Elemental Analysis Unit (SSEAU) within the Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre (MWAC) at UNSW Sydney.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Compositional and morphological characterization of Fe
3
O
4
/rGO composites. (a) Rietveld-refined fit of the XRD data with the Fe3O4 structural model for FG2-400 and FG2-600. (b), (c), and (d) SEM images of FG2-600. (e) C, (f) Fe, and (g) O EDX mapping of FG2-600 corresponding to the SEM image (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Synthesis and physicochemical attributes. (a) Fe wt% in all samples confirmed by ICPMS. (b) The electrical conductivity of all samples was measured via a four-probe method. (c) C1s XPS spectra of G-400 and FG2-400. (d) Schematic of the formation of Fe3O4/rGO aerogels. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of Fe
3
O
4
/rGO composites. (a) CV curves of FG2-600 for the first four cycles at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. (b) Discharge-charge profiles of the FG2-600 electrode. The comparison between the cyclic performance of (c) FG1-400, FG2-400, FG3-400, G-400, FGM-400, and (d) FG2-400, and FG2-600 at a current density of 100 mA g−1 within the potential window between 0.01 V and 3 V. Cross sectional features of (e) FG2-400 and (f) FG2-600 electrodes. (g) ECSA results for FG2-400 and FG2-600 at a scan rate of 0.03 mV min−1. (h) Rate performance of FG2-400 and FG2-600. (i) Cycling stability of FG2-600 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and 1 A g−1, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Postmortem and mechanistic analysis of Fe
3
O
4
/rGO electrodes. (a) Operando XRD of FG2-600 in the first two cycles. The Nyquist plots of (b) FG1-400, FG2-400, FG3-400, and FGM-400, and (c) FG2-200, FG2-400, and FG2-600. The changes in Nyquist plots of (d) FG2-400 and (e) FG2- 600 after 10 and 50 cycles. SEM images of FG2-600 after 50 cycles from a top view (f) and side view (g), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of the cycling performance at 100 mA g−1 against recently reported work. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Structure-dependent lithium storage characteristics of Fe3O4/rGO aerogels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of New South Wales Sydney

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of New South Wales Sydney

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of New South Wales Sydney

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of New South Wales Sydney, NSW, 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) not only shows distinctive electrochemical properties desirable for battery performance but also presents functionalities that enable the production of free-standing electrodes and allow further functionalization to form composites. When combined with Fe3O4 which possesses a high theoretical capacity, Fe3O4/rGO composites demonstrate enhanced battery performance compared to their individual form. Despite numerous efforts invested in developing high-performance Fe3O4/rGO Li-ion battery anodes, fundamental questions regarding structure-dependent lithium storage behaviour have been lacking experimental support in detail. In this work, we report the synthesis of free-standing Fe3O4/rGO that is highly porous with tunable features to increase the availability of electroactive sites, channels for ion passage, and space for Li accommodation, thus enhancing Li storage capacity and prolonging cycle life. As-prepared Fe3O4/rGO electrodes deliver a high reversible capacity of 1224 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and a retention rate of 82.8% after 1000 cycles at 1 A g−1. These Fe3O4/rGO composites with desirable features, hence, hold promise in applications as anodes in high-power lithium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based materials have shown great potential in Li-ion batteries (LIBs) both as active materials and as a conductive matrix to accommodate other electroactive species [1]. The atomic-thin layer of carbon atoms packed by covalent carbon bonds with a continuous conjugated network gives graphene high mechanical strength and flexibility to accommodate stress generated during battery operations, remarkable electrical conductivity for fast electron transport, and a large surface area for facile ion transport across the electrode-electrolyte interface [2,3]. When stacked, few-layer graphene offers interlayer channels for ion intercalation [4,5]. Therefore, combining graphene with commonly used electrode materials, for example, transition metal oxides (TMOs), has been considered an effective strategy for producing high-performance electrodes in LIBs.

TMOs have been intensively explored as promising anode materials in high-performance lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries because of their higher theoretical capacity (500–1000 mAh g−1) than that of commercial graphite electrodes (372 mAh g−1) [6,7]. Such high capacity values of TMOs originate from the electrochemical conversion reaction that leads to complete conversion of the TMO into the transition metal and Li2O [6,8]. Among all the TMOs, Fe3O4 stands out due to its high theoretical capacity of 924 mA h g−1, abundance, inexpensiveness, and environmental benignity [9–12]. However, like other TMOs, Fe3O4-based anodes suffer from several limitations, including poor electrical conductivity, structural instability due to the large volume change caused by Li+ insertion and extraction, and the tendency of the particles to aggregate over continuous cycling, all of which lead to limited cycling performance of Fe3O4 in practical applications [10,13,14].

In order to resolve these issues, many efforts have been directed at distributing Fe3O4 nanoparticles in various forms of carbon-based systems for the following reasons. 1. The carbon materials typically possess high conductivity, which is critical for rapid and efficient electrode response [15–17], 2. Carbonaceous materials possess excellent mechanical properties, which can help accommodate structural stress generated during cycling, thereby improving the durability of electrodes [15–17], 3. The tunable nanostructure of carbon-based materials, for example, nanosphere [18,19], nanotube [20,21], nanosheet [22,23], and three-dimensional foam [14,24,25], can help realize desirable anode architecture with a large contact area between the active material and the electrolyte, and a shortened diffusion pathway for Li ions. Several strategies have been implemented to further optimize the electrochemical properties of the Fe3O4/carbon system, including doping with heteroatoms and nanostructure design of the composite [26–28]. Apart from battery application, graphene has also been demonstrated as a viable matrix to accommodate functional nanoparticles owing to its high electrical conductivity, large surface area, and outstanding mechanical properties [3,26,27].

Since reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can be produced in bulk quantities, it has been the primary alternative for graphene in the field of energy storage. A typical method to prepare Fe3O4/rGO begins with the synthesis of Fe-precursor-graphene oxide (GO) composites, followed by the annealing process [28–30]. This synthesis route has been commonly used but is complicated and requires the usage of various chemicals, such as urea and sodium acetate. To simplify the process, a hydrothermal route has been proposed, which sidesteps the preparation of precursor composites and typically involves ammonia to avoid the formation of byproducts [23,31–33]. In the hydrothermal method, the reduction of GO and the formation of Fe3O4 occur simultaneously in a liquid medium. It is known that rGO sheets tend to aggregate in aqueous media during the reducing process [34,35], which may result in inhomogeneous dispersion of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [36]. Moreover, the re-stacking of rGO laminates occurs during the charging/discharging process. Such collapse in the exfoliated structure of graphene induces a loss in active sites and impedes the ion diffusion process [37,38]. Consequently, it is challenging to reach the expected capacity for various forms of rGO-based electrodes, such as nanosheets [39] and aerogels [40], in practical applications. Therefore, it is significant to develop a preparation method to avoid aforementioned issues and to design a durable structure that inhibits graphene re-stacking during cycling.

In this work, we present a modified route of designing three-dimensional (3D) Fe3O4/rGO aerogel electrodes, which avoids using additional chemicals, prevents rGO sheets from restacking and Fe3O4 from aggregation by separating the mixing step and the following heat treatment. Notably, as-prepared composites can be directly used as free-standing electrodes without adding binder and conductive agents due to the interconnected high-conductivity network furnished by rGO. The impact of geometric structure on Li cycling performance is investigated by comparing two-dimensional (2D) Fe3O4/rGO membranes with 3D Fe3O4/rGO aerogels. Moreover, microscale adjustments in structure have been made to gain an integrated understanding of the structure-dependent Li cycling behaviour of Fe3O4/rGO composites. As a result, the highly and hierarchically porous Fe3O4/rGO electrodes showcase high capacity (1224 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1) and excellent cycling stability (a retention rate of 82.8% at 1 A g−1 after 1000 cycles).

GO, synthesized via a modified Hummer's method, was supplied by NiSiNa Materials Japan. The concentration of GO dispersion in deionized water is 1 mg ml−1. After adding various amounts of FeCl3 powder into GO, the mixture was sonicated for 3 h to form a uniformly dispersed suspension and then freeze-dried to obtain aerogel samples. Subsequently, these aerogels were heated in an N2 atmosphere at varied temperatures ranging from 200 °C to 600 °C for the purpose of reducing GO and oxidizing FeCl3. The final aerogel product is denoted as, for example, FG1-400, where FG represents Fe3O4/rGO, 1 is the weight ratio of FeCl3 and GO applied in the preparation process, and 400 corresponds to the annealing temperature of 400 °C. For comparison, Fe3O4/rGO membranes were made by filtrating FeCl3/GO mixture through a polyvinylidene fluoride (0.22 μm) membrane under vacuum and then peeling off. These composite membranes were subsequently thermally treated at 400 °C under N2, and the resultant membranes are denoted as FGM-400.

The surface morphology and elemental distribution of samples before and after cycling were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM 450) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 fitted with an SDD-EDS detector), respectively. The thickness of these free-standing electrodes was measured based on the cross-sectional images captured by SEM. The X-ray diffraction technique (XRD, PANalytical Empyrean 1 Cu Source) was used to analyze the phase composition of FG composites. The phase evolution of the electrode during battery cycling was probed by Operando XRD using a homemade cell equipped with a glassy carbon window. FexOy to rGO weight ratio in the composites was confirmed by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS, Optima7000 and Avio from PerkinElmer, USA), and by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, PerkinElmer Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer 8000) performed under O2 atmosphere from room temperature (22 °C) to 800 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Al-Κα radiation) was used to investigate the chemical composition of the samples. The electrical conductivity was measured by a four-probe method (Ossila four-point probe).

LIB half-cells (CR2032 coin cells) were assembled to test the electrochemical performance of the samples as potential anode materials. After mechanically pressing with a hydraulic laminating press (MTI Corporation), Fe3O4/rGO aerogels transformed into free-standing thin films, which were then punched into 12 mm discs and transferred to a vacuum oven to remove residual moisture. These thin films were then directly used as the electrode with Li metal as the counter electrode. 1 M LiPF6 in 1:1 (volume) mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) was employed as the electrolyte. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) cycling was performed on a Neware BTS battery cycler. Cyclic voltammetry measurements (CV, BioLogic VMP-3e) were conducted from 0.01 V to 3 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, BioLogic VMP-3e) was conducted by applying an alternating current perturbation of 10 mV in 1 MHz–100 mHz frequency range. Prior to the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) measurement, samples were discharged-charged one time and rested till the equilibrium was established. ECSA measurements were followed at a scan rate of 0.03 mV min−1 within 10 mV potential window with respect to the open-circuit voltage (OCV).

The phases and crystal structure of the materials were analyzed by XRD, as presented in Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. S1. Rietveld analysis was conducted using a structural model of Fe3O4 and XRD data with the program GSASII [41]. Background, zero offset, lattice and atomic parameters were refined, and the size term was refined in order to estimate the particle size distribution from the measured peak shapes (Table S1). All samples, except for FG2-200, display a set of diffraction peaks located at 30.7, 36.0, 43.6, 54.0, 57.5, and 63.1°, indexed to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of the spinel Fe3O4 phase. The broad peak centered at around 25° corresponds to the (002) plane or the interlayer distance of rGO. However, when annealed at 200 °C, a mixture of phases consisting of Fe3O4, FeOOH, and iron chloride hydroxides (Fig. S1(a)), forms as a result of incomplete reaction of FeCl3 with GO. The morphology of these Fe3O4/rGO products was characterized by SEM. It can be seen in Fig. 1(b) that FG2-600 presents a structure of high porosity where nanoflakes containing open pores pile loosely to form a hierarchically porous network. These pores on rGO laminates result from the gas emitted by the decomposition of functional groups on GO during the annealing process [42,43]. Such a porous network provides ample channels for electrolytes and, hence, ions to percolate into the bulk of the composite; in this regard, a higher annealing temperature for FG2-600 supposedly introduces more pores compared to FG2-400 (Fig. S2(a)and (b)). Fig. 1(c) shows that Fe3O4 nanoparticles are uniformly dispersed on the rGO surface without any obvious evidence of particle clustering or aggregation. EDX results in Fig. 1(d)–(g) confirm the uniform distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles within the composite system.

Overall, a hierarchically porous rGO matrix with a homogeneous distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles is formed, which can provide interconnected ion diffusion channels and adequate buffer space to resist volume change during cycling. Additionally, the high surface area derived from the 3D rGO layer enables a large contact area between the electrode and electrolyte. To explore the influence of rGO arrangement at the macroscale on the battery performance, 2D Fe3O4/rGO membranes were prepared and compared against the 3D aerogels. Different from the randomly aligned laminates in Fe3O4/rGO aerogels, Fe3O4/rGO membrane (Fig. S2(c)) presents a typical wrinkled paper-like structure of rGO, more compact than its aerogel counterpart. Fig. S2(d)–(f) evidenced the homogeneous dispersion of Fe3O4 in the membrane.

The weight ratio of Fe contents in composites ranges from 10 wt% to 29 wt%, as quantitively measured by ICPMS(Fig. 2 (a)) and TGA (Table S2 and Fig. S3). The conductivity of the electrodes was measured using a four-probe method (Fig. 2(b)) after aerogels were compressed in pellet form. Compared with the electrical conductivity of pure rGO aerogel (9.3 S m−1), the incorporation of Fe3O4 improved the electrical conductivity of the composite, and the electrical conductivity increased with increasing Fe3O4 loading. As reported previously, the higher porosity of graphene foam leads to higher activation energy for the conduction process [44]. Hence, despite the low electrical conductivity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (0.3 S m−1) [45], the added Fe3O4 in the rGO framework acts as a physical crosslinker in the aerogel and, therefore, reduces the resistance at junction areas in rGO laminates [46,47]. Interestingly, the electrical conductivity of FG2-600 is lower than that of FG2-400. Although it is known that a high temperature contributes to restoring the conductive network of rGO by removing residual functionalities on rGO, the presence of excessive pores in FG2-600, compared with FG2-400, negatively impacts the electrical conductivity [44,46]. The 2D FGM-400 shows the highest conductivity probably due to its compact configuration.

XPS was performed to examine the variation in functional groups in these composites, as shown in Fig. 2(c) and Fig. S4. Compared with pure rGO aerogel G-400, FG2-400 shows an increase in CC C 1s and a decrease in C–O and CO C 1s signal, indicating that oxygenated groups on GO act as anchors to produce Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Upon increasing the iron (FeCl3) loading, Fe3O4 weight ratio in aerogel composites increases with a slight increase in Cl residue (Fig. S4(a)). On elevating the heating temperature from 400 °C to 600 °C, higher temperature intensifies the thermal decomposition of GO, and thus leaves more Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. S4(b)). Combining with the XPS results, the preparation process of Fe3O4/rGO aerogels, the schematic of which is demonstrated in Fig. 2(d), can thus be clarified as follows. The Fe3O4/rGO aerogels were prepared via a modified method that involved the aerogel-making from a mixture of GO and an iron salt, and the thermal treatment of such aerogels. When dispersed in water, oxygenated functional groups on GO render GO sheets negatively charged, thus attracting Fe3+ cations to be attached. After being transformed into aerogels via freeze-drying, GO was thermally reduced by decomposing oxygen-containing groups into gases under N2 atmosphere, and simultaneously, FeCl3 reacts with oxygen-containing groups on GO, mostly epoxy and hydroxyl groups, leading to the formation of iron oxide. In the hydrothermal treatment, the most common method of producing Fe3O4/rGO composites, rGO sheets tend to agglomerate to lower the surface energy and may restack, which could lead to the inhomogeneous arrangement of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the sheets. Ma et al. reported the synthesis of the metal oxide/rGO composite using hydrothermal and freeze-drying methods, respectively [36]. The variation in morphology was compared and analyzed via SEM and TEM imaging. Freeze-dried samples display thin layers of rGO with uniformly dispersed nanoparticles, whereas hydrothermally synthesized rGO appears in a bulkier form, and nanoparticles agglomerate on rGO layers. Thus, our method allows the formation of a highly porous rGO network and a uniform distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles through Fe3+ anchoring onto GO sheets and the reduction of GO.

The electrochemical behaviour of all samples was examined by performing CV and GCD tests. Fig. 3 (a) presents the CV curves of the FG2-600 electrode during the first four cycles in the 0.01–3 V voltage window at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. In the first cathodic process, a sharp peak is observed at 0.55 V, which is assigned to the electrochemical conversion reaction of Fe3O4, and the irreversible formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) by electrolyte decomposition. The current response near 0.01 V corresponds to the Li intercalation into rGO sheets [4]. The first anodic process exhibits two broad peaks located at 1.1 V and 1.7 V, which are attributed to the oxidation of Fe0 into Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. The reversible reactions occurring in the battery are expressed as below:
(1)
F e
3
O 4
+ 8
L i
+
+ 8
e −
↔ 3
F e
0
+ 4
L i
2
O
(2)
C
(
r G O
)
+ x
L i
+
+ x
e −
↔
L i
x
C

In the subsequent scans, both the reduction and oxidation peaks are shifted to a higher voltage, indicating the transformation of the crystalline iron oxide during the first cycle. It can be observed that CV curves of FG2-600 practically overlapped after the first two cycles, highlighting the excellent cycling reversibility of FG2-600.

The influence of pore structure on the battery performance of Fe3O4/rGO composites as anodes was investigated via the controlled variation of the composition and annealing temperature. GCD tests were performed by applying a constant current density of 100 mA g−1 in the 0.01 V–3 V potential window. Fig. 3(b) presents the galvanostatic cycling profiles of the FG2-600 electrode. The profile, which is representative of all Fe3O4/rGO electrodes (Fig. S5), changes considerably after the first cycle, where the electrolyte decomposition-mediated SEI formation appears in a plateau at around 0.75 V [24]. Afterwards, the profile becomes fully reversible, as evident from the overlaying charge and discharge traces from 2nd to 50th cycle. Nevertheless, a high reversible capacity of 1364 mAh g−1 is obtained in the 2nd cycle, which drops marginally to 1048 mAh g−1 after 50 cycles. The high specific capacity of FG2-600 has exceeded the theoretical value for either Fe3O4 (924 mAh g−1) or rGO (744 mAh g−1). In fact, such extra Li storage is commonly observed for transition metal oxide anodes. According to Li et al., this is because the electrochemically reduced Fe particles enable the excessive storage of spin-polarized electrons and result in large surface capacitance [48]. To assess the contribution of the rGO to the specific capacity displayed by the FG2-600 electrode, pure rGO aerogel electrodes were cycled under the same conditions (Fig. S6(a) and (b)). Typically, G-600 delivers an initial charging capacity of 434.6 mAh g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency of 57.6%, but the capacity rapidly decreases to 247.8 mAh g−1 after 30 cycles. The low initial Coulombic efficiency owes to the SEI formation during the first discharge (reduction). The subsequent decay in capacity, which is commonly observed in graphene-based electrodes, can be attributed to the restacking of graphene sheets and, hence, the loss of active sites for Li diffusion and storage. Therefore, it can be said that the specific capacity displayed by FG2-600 after 50th cycle primarily originates from the redox cycling of the Fe3O4 and the capacity loss between the 2nd and the 50th cycle can be attributed to the capacity loss for rGO. Hoowever, to evaluate the effect of the relative iron content in the electrode, FGX-400 electrodes with X = 1, 2 and 3 were galvanostatically cycled. The corresponding cycling data is shown in Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S6. Compared to the bare rGO electrode, all aerogel electrodes loaded with Fe3O4 show an enhanced specific capacity, and it increases with the increasing weight ratio of Fe3O4 from FG1-400 to FG2-400 but decreases for FG3-400. This observation explains the role of the high surface area rGO network on the electrochemical activity of the composite aerogel electrode. At a high Fe3O4 content (FG3-400), the smaller fraction of rGO cannot buffer the electromechanical stress generated from the large volume change associated with Fe3O4 cycling, and the composite behaves like pure Fe3O4 and displays inferior electrochemical performance [49]. The cycling data in Fig. 3(c) thus indicates that both rGO and Fe3O4 play a synergistic role in determining Li storage capacity. Among all, FG2-400 is identified as the optimal electrode that delivers not only the highest initial reversible capacity of 1364.4 mAh g−1, three times that of the rGO electrode, but also a higher initial Coulombic efficiency of 73.8% (Fig. S6(c)). 2D Fe3O4/rGO membranes were also tested to understand the influence of composite microstructure structure on battery performance. It can be seen (Fig. 3(c)) that the specific capacity of FGM is far from that of any aerogel, which demonstrates that 3D aerogel structure at the macroscale is more favorable for the lithiation/de-lithiation process.

Fig. 3(d) compares the cycling performance of FG2-400 and FG2-600, the two high-performing aerogel electrodes, and sheds light on the role of the microstructure in affecting the electrochemical performance. FG2-600, with a lower Fe loading of 19.2 wt%, despite a lower capacity in the first few cycles, shows a better capacity retention than FG2-400. After 100 cycles, FG2-600 displays a capacity of 1053 mAh g−1 with a retention rate of 86.9% compared to 74.5% retention and 1016.3 mAh g−1 for FG2-400. With respect to the electronic conductivity, which is higher for FG2-400 than FG2-600, the capacity and cyclability data are counterintuitive. The origin of FG2-600's better performance lies in its microscopic structure, which was further investigated in detail. The cross-sectional morphology of Fe3O4/rGO aerogels in Fig. 3(e) and (f), and Fig. S7 offer a comprehensive insight into the impact of the annealing process on the resulting composite structure. Clearly, the thickness of the aerogel increases upon increasing the annealing temperature from 400 °C to 600 °C even though the same mass loading and composition were applied in the preparation step. The thickness of the aerogel changes from 68.2 μm for FG2-400 to 207.3 μm for FG2-600, accompanied by a structural transformation from a densely packed arrangement to a loosely built construction, which can be further confirmed by the SEM images at higher magnification (inset). Evidently, it can be seen in Fig. 3(e) and (f) that the stacking of rGO layers is present in FG2-400 while most rGO sheets are exfoliated into fewer layers in FG2-600. This decrease in rGO lamellar structure at elevated temperatures stems from higher pressure generated during the decomposition of oxygenated functional groups at a higher temperature, leaving rGO sheets more exfoliated. This fewer-layered configuration ensures a heightened exposure of electrode materials to the electrolyte and prevents the restacking of rGO layers, thus resulting in an efficient utilization of active materials in battery reactions and a well-maintained structure after cycling. ECSA measurements on FG2-400 and FG-600 in Fig. 3(g) also quantitatively evidence that the availability of electroactive sites exceeds in FG2-600 than that in FG2-400. Apparently, the expanded space in FG2-600 as ion diffusion shortcuts could lead to an unexpected loss in active materials per unit volume. It is typical that such porous electrodes suffer from low tap density, which in turn negatively impacts the volumetric energy density of the electrode [50]. Although this work focuses on gravimetric energy density, it should be noted that for practical applications volumetric energy density would need to be optimized for such a material system. FG2-200 shows the lowest charge capacity of 753.9 mAh g−1 in the first cycle, rapidly decays to 434.7 mAh g−1 after 10 cycles, and then remains stable in the following cycles (Fig. S6(e)). This poor performance can be ascribed to the incomplete reaction between the Fe3+ and GO at 200 °C and the mixture of phases as indicated by the XRD pattern (Fig. S1(a)), which are electrochemically less active and less stable for Li+ cycling. FG1 samples, which were thermally treated from 200 °C to 600 °C, show a similar capacity trend as that for FG2 samples (Fig. S6(f)).

Given the enhanced electrochemical performance of FG2-400 and FG2-600, these electrodes were selected to analyze their rate capability, as shown in Fig. 3(h) and (i). At lower current rates, FG2-400 shows a higher capacity than FG2-600. However, the Li storage capacity of FG2-600 exceeds that of FG2-400, when the current density goes above 0.5 A g−1. The improved performance of FG2-600 at higher rates is probably because the highly porous structure with interconnected channels allows easy access to the electrolyte and facile Li+ diffusion pathways. When testing the durability of electrodes at a higher rate, such as 0.5 A g−1 and 1 A g−1, the electrode suffers from rapid volumetric change, and the resultant stress increases the polarization. As shown in Fig. 3(i), the capacity of FG2-600 initially increases with cycling due to the activation process of iron oxides. After 1000 cycles, FG2-600 delivers a capacity of 615.2 mAh g−1 and 575.2 mAh g−1, with a retention rate of 85.2% and 82.8%, at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 and 1 A g−1, respectively, indicating the excellent structural integrity maintained over the long-term cycling.

To further understand the electrochemical reaction mechanism, the evolution of FG2-600 was probed in operando by XRD during the battery cycling. The data is illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). As expected for the electrochemical conversion mechanism proposed above, the peaks corresponding to Fe3O4 quickly fade during the first discharge. After the first cycle, aside from the sharp peaks from the cell body, only the rGO peak is left, indicating that the active Fe3O4 converts to an amorphous phase, typical of all conversion-type oxide anode materials [49]. Ex situ XRD of the FG2-600 electrode cycled 10 times and 50 times further confirms the operando XRD observation (Fig. S8).

To gain further insights into the electrode process, the composite electrodes were probed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy or EIS. Nyquist impedance data of all electrodes, as shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), consists of a depressed semi-circle in the high-to-medium frequency region, corresponding to the charge transfer process (resistance: RCT) at the electrode-electrolyte interface and an inclined line in the low-frequency region, corresponding to the solid-state diffusion of lithium within active materials. The RCT of all composites is listed in Table S3 and the EIS equivalent circuit is demonstrated in Fig. S9. FGM-400 possesses an RCT of 704.4 Ω, which is higher than its aerogel counterparts. This result demonstrates that the porous aerogel microstructure enables a large electrode-electrolyte contact area and, thus, fast ion transport across the interface. As for aerogel samples, RCT varies with the Fe3O4 mass loading and the annealing temperature. FG2-400 displays the lowest RCT of 296.6 Ω, most likely owing to the combination of its high electronic conductivity and the optimal microstructure, and the RCT is consistent with its galvanostatic performance.

The comparison between the EIS data of FG2-400 and FG2-600 after cycling, as shown in Fig. 4(d) and (e), respectively, reveals the origin of the stable long-term cycling of FG2-600. The RCT of FG2-600 dramatically reduces from 580.3 Ω before cycling to 54.6 Ω after 10 cycles and 40.7 Ω after 50 cycles, which implies the significantly facilitated redox kinetics. Although the specific mechanism for this improvement in charge transfer kinetics is unclear and needs further exploration, it is believed to result from the activation of iron oxide nanoparticles that are homogeneously distributed in the optimal 3D porous network formed by rGO. Since structural integrity is a pivotal factor in achieving the durability of electrode materials, the morphology of cycled electrodes was probed by SEM and shown in Fig. 4(f) and (g), and Fig. S10. After 50 cycles, the highly porous structure of FG2-600 still maintains, and rGO laminates remain well-exfoliated without obvious evidence of re-stacking laminates. Fig. 4(f) shows that crystalline Fe3O4 nanoparticles have most likely changed into an amorphous form as indicated by the XRD patterns of cycled electrodes and consistent with the CV profiles. EDX in Fig. S10 evidences the uniform distribution of Fe after 50 cycles.

To evidence the excellent battery performance demonstrated by the FG2-600 composite electrode, Fig. 5 compares it against the electrochemical performance of Fe3O4/graphene-based LIB anodes reported in the literature in terms of the reversible capacity obtained in the 1st and 100th cycle at 100 mA g−1 [14,24,51–54]. Various forms of Fe3O4/rGO composites, ranging from 0D to 3D, have been studied in search of the optimal structure for Li storage [14,24,51–54] and the electrochemical performance of FG2-600 is evidently outstanding, especially considering its facile preparation process. This work compared the battery performance of 2D and 3D Fe3O4/rGO electrodes and showed the enhanced Li cycling performance of 3D aerogel due to its flexibility and ample access to active materials. Based on 3D aerogel structure of FG2-400, we further optimized the microstructure (FG2-600) by adjusting the thermal annealing process. The tailored microstructure of FG2-600 clearly has a positive impact on Li cycling especially at high current densities and on maintaining the electrode integrity for long-term cycling.

In this work, we provide insights into the structure-dependent Li storage behaviour in Fe3O4/rGO composites. That is, the geometric structure determines the Li storage capacity, while the microscale structure affects fast charging behaviour and electrode durability. Firstly, we compared the Li cycling behaviour of 3D Fe3O4/rGO aerogel with 2D membranes. Our results suggest that the 3D network, on the one hand, is more favoured for Li storage; on the other hand, it does not necessarily suppress capacity degradation. Secondly, the microscopic structure of 3D Fe3O4/rGO has been further tuned via the annealing process. Compared to FG2-400, a more porous structure of FG2-600 is constructed with more exfoliated rGO laminates. FG2-600 not only shows enhanced Li capacity at high current densities but also reduces capacity decay. Our study suggests that the facile synthesis route and the adopted structure design are possible options for the scalable production of transition metal oxide/carbon composites for high-performance electrodes.

Fei Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xinyue Wen: Methodology, Investigation. Uttam Mittal: Investigation. Rasoul Khayyam Nekouei: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Tobias Foller: Investigation. Yuan Shang: Investigation. Abhirup Bhadra: Investigation. Dewei Chu: Resources, Investigation. Neeraj Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis. Dipan Kundu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Rakesh Joshi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Fei Wang acknowledges Australian research training program. The authors acknowledge the facilities and the scientific and technical assistance of Microscopy Australia at the Electron Microscope Unit (EMU), Spectroscopy Laboratory and Solid State & Elemental Analysis Unit (SSEAU) within the Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre (MWAC) at UNSW Sydney.

Sci. 8 (2015) 2371-2376. https://doi.org/10.1039/c5ee01363a.
